name: Conventional cotton
note: Footprint varies hugely by source and region (~1.2–16 kg CO₂e/kg). The defining impacts are enormous water use and a heavy pesticide/fertiliser load.

name: Virgin polyester
note: Cheap and low-water, but fossil-derived, sheds microplastics every wash and is effectively non-biodegradable.

name: Virgin nylon
note: Production can release nitrous oxide, a greenhouse gas ~270× as potent as CO₂. Strong and water-resistant, but fossil-based and microplastic-shedding.

name: Acrylic
note: Fossil-based 'fake wool'. Sheds microplastics heavily and can persist ~200 years in landfill.

name: Conventional wool
note: Estimates range wildly (~5–80 kg CO₂e/kg) depending on how sheep methane is allocated — rumination dominates. A prime example of why allocation method, not just material, drives the number.

name: Conventional viscose / rayon
note: Per-kg CO₂ is moderate, but the real harm is the toxic solvent process (caustic soda, carbon disulfide) and links to deforestation. Cleaner versions are sold as lyocell/Tencel.

name: Patacel (Fibe)
note: Fibre from potato stems and leaves — a genuine waste stream. Vendor figures: 82% less CO₂ and 99.7% less water than cotton, at a competitive ~£1,300/tonne. Early-stage, not yet at full commercial scale, so figures are vendor-reported.

name: Organic cotton
note: Lower chemical inputs than conventional cotton, but yields per hectare can be lower and it remains water-intensive.

name: Recycled cotton
note: Big water and energy savings vs virgin cotton, but recycling shortens fibres and cuts durability — so it's usually blended with virgin fibre.

name: Tencel / lyocell
note: Lyocell made in a closed-loop solvent process that recovers ~99% of chemicals. Impact hinges on responsibly-sourced pulp (FSC/PEFC certified).

name: Hemp
note: Very durable, low-input and improves soil, but has a coarser hand-feel and figures vary a lot by retting/processing route.

name: Linen (flax)
note: Flax has one of the lowest fibre footprints — it grows with little irrigation or pesticide. Undyed linen is biodegradable; downside is it wrinkles easily.

name: Recycled polyester (rPET)
note: Roughly 40–50% lower CO₂ than virgin polyester, but it still sheds microplastics and isn't biodegradable. Mechanical recycling degrades the fibre over cycles.

name: Recycled nylon (Econyl-type)
note: Regenerated from waste nylon such as ghost fishing nets. Vendors claim up to ~90% lower at the raw-material stage; full-LCA savings are smaller, and it still sheds microplastics.

name: Recycled wool
note: Skips sheep farming entirely, sidestepping wool's biggest impact (methane). Fibre is shorter and weaker, so it's usually blended with virgin wool.

name: Bamboo lyocell
note: Bamboo grows fast with little input, BUT most 'bamboo' fabric is chemically-intensive viscose. Only closed-loop bamboo lyocell is genuinely cleaner — check which one you're buying.

name: Orange Fiber
note: Made from 'pastazzo', the citrus pulp left after juicing. A genuine waste-stream story, but production is small-scale and independent LCA data is scarce.

name: SeaCell
note: A lyocell fibre infused with seaweed, often marketed for skin benefits. Carbon data is largely vendor-reported, hence low confidence.

name: Bovine leather
note: Hugely allocation-dependent. Under economic allocation — which assigns only ~2% of cattle impact to the hide — peer-reviewed LCAs land at ~12–22 kg CO₂e/m² (Zhou et al. 11.6; Carbonfact 22.48). On a full-boundary basis that includes cattle rearing it is ~73–110 kg/m², the figure used here. A top-down meta-analysis goes higher still, ~110–190 kg CO₂e per kg of finished leather. Cattle rearing dominates either way; chrome tanning adds hazardous wastewater, so 'biodegradable' is heavily qualified. Extremely durable. The allocation method, not the material, drives most of this spread.

name: Cactus leather (Desserto)
note: The only LCA available is Desserto's own (ISO 14040/44 but early-stage, maker-funded, run on the least-PU ≈30%-bio version): it reports ~1.4 kg CO₂e/m². The 4 used here is deliberately more conservative. Commercial material is ~25–30% bio-PU, so true biodegradability is limited, and the widely-quoted 'carbon-negative farm' claim is a farm-level figure that excludes the PU/polyester. Slightly less durable than animal leather.

name: Grape leather (Vegea)
note: 55–65% bio-based, from grape skins, seeds and stalks left after winemaking. No published independent LCA — treat figures with real caution.

name: Pineapple leather (Piñatex)
note: Uses leaves left after the pineapple harvest and can be near carbon-neutral at the fibre stage, but it's typically coated with a petroleum-based resin.

name: Apple leather (AppleSkin-type)
note: Around a third apple-waste pulp, the rest rubber/PU and bio-binders. A genuine waste feedstock, but the synthetic content limits biodegradability.

name: Mycelium leather (Mylo-type)
note: 'Mycelium leather' is really two materials. Active, CO₂-fed growth (Mylo-type) is energy-intensive — the ~57 kg CO₂e/m² used here. Passive growth (MycoWorks' Reishi) is roughly two orders of magnitude lower: its peer-reviewed but maker-funded LCA reports ~2.76 kg CO₂e/m². So the verdict versus animal leather flips entirely on which process AND which bovine benchmark you pick — 57 beats the full-boundary bovine figure (~100) but loses to low-end estimates (~22). A vivid lesson in how process route and chosen benchmark, not 'is it natural?', decide the answer. The 57 figure's provenance is weak; treat as indicative.

name: Cork leather
note: Harvested from cork oaks without felling them (the forests are carbon sinks). Naturally water-resistant and durable with a distinctive texture; limited colour range.

name: PU synthetic 'leather'
note: Lowest CO₂/m² and cheapest of the alternatives, but fossil-based, sheds microplastics and has a shorter lifespan — the 'cheap fake leather' benchmark.
